name: Ali'iolani Hale & King Kamehameha Statue
tagline: The iconic King Kamehameha I statue stands before Hawaii's original government building — draped in lei every June 11 for King Kamehameha Day.
parking: Metered street parking on King St
hours: Exterior and statue always accessible; Judiciary History Center inside open Mon-Fri 8am-4pm
bestTime: June 11 (King Kamehameha Day) when the statue is draped in enormous lei — one of Hawaii's most iconic sights
talkStory: Designed by Australian architect Thomas Rowe in 1872 for Kamehameha V as a palace, Ali'iolani Hale was completed after his death and became the Kingdom's government building instead. The 1893 overthrow of the Hawaiian monarchy was staged from its front steps. The famous Kamehameha statue out front is actually a replica — the original was lost at sea off the Falkland Islands, later recovered, and now stands in Kapa'au on the Big Island. On June 11, volunteers drape 25-foot lei using cherry pickers in a ceremony that draws thousands.
